qa_pairs.question: How do users manage software environments on Trillium using module commands?
qa_pairs.answer: Users on Trillium can manage software environments using various module commands: `module load <module-name>` loads the default version of a package, or `module load <module-name>/<module-version>` loads a specific version. `module purge` unloads all currently loaded modules, `module avail` lists available modules, and `module list` shows currently loaded modules. To search for modules and their dependencies, `module spider` or `module spider <module-name>` can be used. Handy abbreviations like `ml` for `module list` and `ml <module-name>` for `module load <module-name>` are also available.

qa_pairs.question: What are the recommended practices for managing software modules on Trillium to ensure reproducibility and avoid conflicts?
qa_pairs.answer: To ensure reproducibility and avoid conflicts when managing software modules on Trillium, it is recommended to avoid loading modules in your `.bashrc` file. Instead, modules should be loaded manually, from a separate script, or by using module collections, ideally inside your job script. Users should also be explicit about module versions (e.g., `gcc/13.3` instead of just `gcc`) to prevent future changes, and use `module spider <module-name>` to resolve any module dependencies.

qa_pairs.question: How can users test and debug their code on Trillium before submitting a full job to the cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: Users can conduct lightweight tests directly on the Trillium login nodes, provided these tests run in under a few minutes, use no more than 1–2 GB of memory, utilize only 1–4 CPU cores, and at most 1 GPU. For tests exceeding these login node limits or requiring dedicated resources, users can request an interactive debug job using the `debugjob` command on a login node. This grants an interactive shell on a CPU compute session for 1 hour (from a CPU login node) or an interactive session with 1 GPU on a shared GPU compute node for 2 hours (from a GPU login node). The parallel ARM DDT debugger is also available after loading `module load ddt-cpu` or `module load ddt-gpu`.
